nativeRange.habitat: Found on lagoon and seaward reefs at depths of 1–15 m. The Percula Clownfish lives in an obligate symbiotic relationship with host anemones — it cannot survive long in the wild without one — and associates primarily with Heteractis magnifica and Stichodactyla gigantea, occasionally with Heteractis crispa.

diet.notes: Accepts most prepared foods. A varied diet of high-quality marine pellets, flakes, and enriched frozen foods (mysis, brine) is generally advised to support coloration and immune function.

imageCandidates.notes: Emőke Dénes, 3902x2601. Tack-sharp side profile of a vibrant orange individual showing all three white bars thickly outlined in black and black-margined fins; a second fish behind conveys the pair-bonded social structure.
Christian Gloor, 2048x2048. Striking sharp frontal view nestled in a host anemone, fins spread, showing the black-outlined white head bar and vibrant orange; faint corner photographer watermark.
ARC Centre of Excellence for Coral Reef Studies, ~1024px. Clean side profile in a natural host anemone illustrating the obligate symbiosis; correct three-bar percula pattern, vibrant and in focus.

summary: The Percula Clownfish (Amphiprion percula) is a small, peaceful anemonefish from the western Pacific, widely regarded as one of the most beginner-friendly marine fish. Captive-bred specimens are broadly available, including a range of designer color morphs.
careNotes:
Trade names like 'Semi Picasso', 'Picasso', 'Extreme Picasso', 'Platinum', and 'Onyx' refer to selectively bred color and pattern variants, graded by the amount and shape of white markings. The Semi Picasso grade shows irregular white patches outlined in black, but carries less white than a standard Picasso — incomplete bands rather than fully developed ones. The standard Picasso grade has more extensive irregular white patches, while the Extreme Picasso is the most heavily white-marked of the three and is described by suppliers as producing unique markings on every individual fish. The Platinum Percula is a designer morph developed by ORA through years of selective breeding from the Picasso line; mature specimens have a near-completely white body with essentially none of the species' typical orange-and-black striping, and develop black fins and an orange-tinged face as they grow. None of these morphs occur in the wild, and all share the same husbandry requirements as the wild form.

The Percula Clownfish is generally considered one of the more beginner-friendly marine fish. It is best kept singly or as a mated pair. The species tends to be aggressive toward other clownfish species and toward unfamiliar individuals of its own kind, so pairs are best introduced as juveniles or purchased as an already-bonded pair. A host anemone is not required in captivity, though the fish will readily accept one if a compatible species is offered — typically Heteractis magnifica, Stichodactyla gigantea, or sometimes carpet or bubble-tip anemones.

Like all clownfish, the Percula Clownfish is a protandrous sequential hermaphrodite, meaning every individual is born male. In a social group, the largest fish transitions to become the dominant female, while the second-largest remains the breeding male. The remaining individuals in the group are non-breeding subordinates, and this size-based hierarchy is maintained strictly throughout the group's life.
breedingNotes: The Percula Clownfish has been bred extensively in captivity and is widely regarded as one of the easier marine fish to spawn. Pairs lay adhesive eggs directly on a hard surface near their host anemone rather than scattering them into open water, and the male tends the clutch until hatching. The larvae hatch as free-floating young that drift in the water column and require very small live foods — rotifers are typically offered as a first food, followed by enriched brine shrimp nauplii as the larvae grow.

primary.name: aquaticsunlimited.com
primary.notes: Species page for the Extreme Picasso variant; provided the captive husbandry parameters (size, temperature, pH, salinity, tank size, diet, temperament, care level).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Pomacentridae), wild distribution (Queensland, Melanesia, GBR, New Guinea, Solomon Islands, Vanuatu), wild temperature range (25.5–28.4°C, mean 27.3°C), depth (1–15 m), and host-anemone associations.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Orange clownfish)
additional.notes: Confirmed maximum length 11 cm (avg 8 cm), breeding-female tenure ~12 years, protandrous sex-change biology, and pair-bonded social structure with size-based hierarchy.

additional.name: LiveAquaria
additional.notes: Manifest primary source; the collections page is a catalog index rather than a species page and did not list per-species husbandry details. Cross-verification fell to FishBase + Wikipedia.

additional.name: saltwateraquarium.com
additional.notes: Merge source for the 'Platinum Percula Clownfish' designer-morph trade name. Confirmed species is Amphiprion percula. Confirmed ORA developed the Platinum Percula through selective breeding from their Picasso Clownfish line, that mature specimens have a near-totally white body with no remaining orange-and-black striping, and that they develop black fins and an orange face as they mature. No husbandry parameters provided; trade name was already present in alsoKnownAs and is now described in careNotes.

additional.name: Sea & Reef Aquaculture + LiveAquaria (Semi Picasso Clownfish)
additional.notes: Combined merge sources for the 'Semi Picasso Clownfish' trade name. LiveAquaria's Semi Picasso Clownfish product page (https://www.liveaquaria.com/product/3334/) confirms the variant is Amphiprion percula and describes it as 'orange with irregularly shaped white patches outlined in black' with less white than the standard Picasso grade; captive-bred. Sea & Reef Aquaculture's Picasso clownfish page (https://www.seaandreef.com/marine-ornamental-fish/clownfish/picasso) confirms Picasso grades of A. percula are graded by 'the amount and shape of the white markings' and lists Picasso, Premium Picasso, and Picasso Misbar — 'Semi Picasso' is a retailer designation between Misbar and standard Picasso rather than a Sea & Reef-named grade.

fish.breedingNotes: Pairs spawn readily in mature reef tanks. Rearing the larvae is considered moderately challenging — they require rotifers as a first food before transitioning to enriched Artemia.
